title: Choosing a Regularization Strategy
concept: Choosing a regularization strategy means matching the tool to the failure pattern: weight decay for oversized weights, dropout for co-adapting neurons, L1 for sparsity, and early stopping when validation performance starts to slip.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the clearest tradeoff between L1 and L2?

options.text: L1 encourages sparsity, while L2 encourages smooth shrinkage.
L1 trains only the output layer, while L2 trains only hidden layers.
L1 increases weights, while L2 deletes the loss.

exercises.explain: Exactly. That tradeoff is one of the main clues for choosing between them.

cards.title: A coach's toolkit
cards.body: A good coach does not use the same fix for every athlete. One player needs strength work, another needs rest, another needs better teamwork. Regularization works the same way: different failure patterns call for different tools.

cards.title: Match the tool to the problem
cards.body: Choosing a regularization strategy means asking what kind of overfitting or instability you are seeing. Use weight decay when weights grow too large, dropout when neurons lean too heavily on one another, L1 when you want sparse connections, and early stopping when validation performance starts getting worse even though training keeps improving.

cards.title: Four ways to manage a garden
cards.body: Weight decay is like trimming every plant a little so none grows wild. L1 is cutting some weak stems off entirely. Dropout is rotating which support stakes are available so plants learn to stand more independently. Early stopping is ending the season before late growth becomes messy and unhealthy.

cards.title: A simple decision checklist
cards.body: If validation loss rises after epoch 12, early stopping may be enough. If a few weights keep ballooning, start with L2 weight decay. If you want a compact model with many zero weights, try L1. If hidden units seem to rely too much on specific partner neurons, dropout is a useful extra check.

cards.title: More regularizers is not always better
cards.body: A common myth is that you should stack every regularizer at high strength just to be safe. Too much regularization can cause underfitting. The smarter move is to start with the tool that matches the actual symptom and add more only if the evidence supports it.

cards.title: Default stabilizers vs. special goals
cards.body: Different tools solve different failure patterns, so your first choice should depend on what you observe.

left.text:
Weight decay for oversized weights.
Early stopping when validation starts slipping.

right.text:
Dropout for co-adapting neurons.
L1 when you want exact zero weights and sparsity.

exercises.prompt: Training accuracy keeps improving, but validation accuracy peaks and then starts falling. Which tool best matches that symptom first?

options.text: Increase the number of epochs dramatically.
Force every weight to become larger.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Early stopping is designed for the moment when training keeps improving but generalization starts to slip.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Dropout is especially useful when hidden units rely too heavily on specific partner units during training.
exercises.explain: True. Dropout breaks up that co-adaptation by making the network practice without some units on each pass.

exercises.sentence: If your main goal is exact zero weights and a sparse network, try ___ regularization.
exercises.explain: Correct. L1 is the regularizer most closely associated with exact zeros and sparse connections.

exercises.prompt: Which strategy sounds most well matched to the problem?

options.text: Start with weight decay for giant weights, use dropout if neurons co-adapt, and stop early if validation begins to worsen.
Use every regularizer at maximum strength before looking at any symptoms.
Ignore validation entirely because regularization makes it unnecessary.

exercises.explain: Right. Good regularization is about matching the tool to the observed problem, not turning every dial to the maximum.

exercises.prompt: You are training a small image model. Training accuracy keeps climbing, validation accuracy peaks early, and the hidden units seem to lean too heavily on the same few partner units. You do not need many exact zero weights. What is the best plan?

options.text: Use mild weight decay, add dropout, and stop early once validation performance starts getting worse.
Choose L1 alone because exact sparsity is your main goal.
Train much longer with no regularization so the network can memorize more completely.
Remove the validation split and judge success only by training accuracy.

exercises.explain: Excellent. That plan matches the actual symptoms: weight decay for general control, dropout for co-adaptation, and early stopping when validation starts to slide.
